source.title: Parsing the role of sleep in memory processing

confidence_rationale: Authoritative review of sleep's role in memory consolidation, by a central voice in the field. Argues that sleep-stages-per-se don't process memory; the neuromodulatory and electrophysiological events characterising sleep stages do. Direct R3 anchor for the offline-consolidation arm of bottom-up rule discovery, with cross-reference to existing REE substrate (MECH-272/MECH-285 sleep-related substrates). Review-level rather than primary, hence lower confidence.
failure_signatures: An REE substrate without sleep-replay-style consolidation will fail to reproduce sleep-dependent memory consolidation signatures Stickgold reviews -- specifically, performance gains on procedural / categorical / regularity tasks following sleep that exceed waking-rest controls.

If ARC-064's offline-arm is implemented as a generic batch-update during sleep without modulator-state-specific timing, it will not capture the sleep-stage-specificity Stickgold emphasises (e.g., procedural learning -> REM, declarative -> SWS).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Sleep's role in memory consolidation is real but heterogeneous. Sleep-stages per se do not process memory; the neuromodulatory and electrophysiological events characterising those stages mediate sleep-dependent processing. Different memory types (procedural, declarative, emotional) have different sleep-dependent profiles, and the consolidation operations are stage-specific (e.g., REM for procedural skill consolidation, slow-wave-sleep for declarative consolidation).
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-064's offline-arm is supported by an extensive sleep-consolidation literature; this review supplies the conceptual frame. Bottom-up rule discovery's offline component should be implemented with sleep-state-specific timing (analogous to REM-vs-SWS-specific operations in the brain) rather than as a generic batch-update. Cross-reference: REE substrates MECH-272, MECH-273, MECH-274 (sleep-related extensions) and MECH-285 (sleep replay sampler) are the existing partial-analogs; ARC-064's offline-arm may slot into those substrates or need additions.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Stickgold's review is high-level and does not commit to a specific substrate-implementation prescription. The sleep-stage-specificity argument is empirically strong but its application to REE is conceptual rather than direct -- REE's substrate may not have direct REM/SWS analogs. Transfer risk: moderate-to-high because the specific mechanisms (neuromodulator-mediated stage-specific processing) require REE substrate decisions not pinned down by the review.
mapping.source_context: Curr Opin Neurobiol review by Robert Stickgold (Harvard / BIDMC), one of the field's central voices on sleep-memory interactions. Cited >400 times.

confidence_components.notes: Curr Opin Neurobiol review venue (high-quality secondary literature). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the offline-consolidation principle generalises; the specific stage-specific mechanisms require REE substrate commitments. Transfer risk slightly elevated.
